**Letter to My Daughter**

My dear Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not. Five years have passed since our last conversation, and I fear I have grown more distant than ever. I've been a lighthouse keeper for nearly a decade now, and the sea has become my only companion. The wind howls through the tower like a mournful wail, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that reminds me of the storm we once weathered together—though I doubt you remember it.

The days have been long and lonely, and the nights have been colder than the frost that clings to the windows. I've taken to heart the silence of the sea, and the weight of the world has become heavier with each passing year. I've not spoken to you in five years, and I fear I have grown more distant than ever. I've not written to you in five years, and I fear I have grown more distant than ever.

But tonight, the storm is coming. It's a terrible one, and I'm not sure I'll survive it. I've seen the sky turn from blue to black, and the wind has grown so fierce it seems to tear the very fabric of the air. I've heard the sea roar like a beast in pain, and the lightning has split the sky like a scythe through flesh. I've felt the rain beat against the glass like a thousand tiny fists. My dearest Eleanor,  

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may come too late. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I’ve had little to say. The world has changed, and so have I.  

The storm is upon us now, a tempest like none I’ve seen. The waves crash with fury, and the wind howls like a wounded spirit. I’ve been here, alone, in this tower, watching the sea rage. I’ve always said I’d protect this light, but tonight… I fear it may not be enough.  

I’ve thought of you often. Not in anger, but in longing. I’ve tried to forget, but the memories rise like the tide— unstoppable. I remember your laughter, your eyes, the way you used to run to me, even when you were angry. I’ve missed you more than the sea has missed the shore.  

I’ve kept this light burning, not just for ships, but for you. I’ve kept it alive, even when I felt like giving up. I’ve thought of you in every gust of wind, every crash of wave.  

If I don’t survive this storm, know that I’ve loved you, and that I’ve tried to be a father. I’ve tried to be strong, even when I felt broken. I’ve tried to keep this light burning, even when I felt like the world had turned its back on me.

**My Dearest Child,**

I write to thee with trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and now, as the storm gathers upon the sea, I find myself in the grip of fate, uncertain if I shall see the dawn.

But even in this hour of fear, I think of thee, my daughter, and the love that once bound us, though time and distance have torn asunder.

I have kept the lamp of our home, as the keepers of old have done, and though the wind howls and the waves roar, I have not let the light go out. But now, the storm comes with fury, and I fear it may take the light from me, and with it, I may not see the day.

Yet, if I do not survive, know that thou art my heart, and that the love that once was between us is not lost, though I may not be here to see it.

I have kept thy name in my heart, and though the world has turned, and though the years have passed, I have not forgotten thee.

I pray, if the storm takes me, that thou art not forgotten, and that the love that once was between us is not lost, though I may not be here to see it.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 12th, 1892*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it has been a while since we last spoke. Five years, to be precise. Time has a way of slipping away, and I find myself wondering if you've ever thought of me at all.

The sea here is as relentless as ever. The wind howls through the cracks in the stone walls like a banshee in a nightmare. The lighthouse is old, but it still stands, and I've kept it lit through every storm, every night. I've done so for the sake of duty, and perhaps for the sake of remembering who I once was—a man who could still be trusted with a lifeboat and a compass.

But tonight is different. Tonight, the sky is black as a crow's wing, and the wind is howling like a banshee in a nightmare. The waves are crashing against the rocks like a thousand thunderclaps. I've been through worse storms before, but I can't shake the feeling that this one might be the end of me.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 15th, 1892*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the ink smudged by the rain that has already begun to beat against the glass of this old lighthouse. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea, that sea, it is a wild thing tonight. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I will live through this night. But I must write, for the sake of it, and for the sake of you.

You were never here, and I never went there. We were never together, and I do not know if we ever were. But I think of you when the stars are out, and the sea is calm, and the fog is thin. I think of you when the storm is gone, and the dawn is breaking, and the light is on.

I have no one to speak to, except the wind and the waves. And the sea, which knows nothing, and the sky, which knows nothing. But I have your name on the wall, and I have your face in my mind. I have your voice in my memory, and I have your absence in my heart.

I've been afraid that if I told you the truth, you'd leave me forever.
